top_results.snippet: # Affordable Housing Plans & Policies. The City of San José has a strong commitment to ensure that all its residents have decent, safe, and affordable housing. The City prepares various housing-related plans and reports that articulate the priorities and strategies for making San José a great place to call home. ## housing-related policies and ordinances. San José has adopted policies and ordinances to address its housing affordability crisis. Note that these policies and ordinances are administered by the Housing Department, the Department of Planning, Building, and Code Enforcement, and other City departments. **Diridon Affordable Housing Implementation Plan:** A strategy document that takes a 3P's approach on reaching the City's housing-related goals in the Diridon Station Area. To learn more, please visit the Diridon Affordable Housing Implementation Plan webpage. **Affordable Housing Siting Policy:** A policy intended to help the City affirmatively further fair housing while delivering much-needed affordable housing. To learn more, please visit the Affordable Housing Siting Policy webpage.

top_results.snippet: On January 27th, dubbed “Housing Day,” San Jose’s Council will consider significant changes to existing laws and programs that support housing development and affordability. * **Proposal for modified affordability in the Inclusionary Housing Ordinance** (would shift rent requirements on market-rate development for inclusionary units upwards, shorten the time these homes stay affordable, and streamline compliance process). * **The City has not developed an alternative comprehensive plan to address housing affordability in San Jose.** California Government Code Section 65583(c) requires local jurisdictions to “make adequate provision for the housing needs of all economic segments of the community” and “assist in the development of adequate housing to meet the needs of extremely low, very low, low-, and moderate-income households.” The Regional Housing Need Allocation (RHNA) reflects this, with the City of San Jose responsible for 15,088 new very low and extremely low income homes, and another 8,687 new low income homes.

top_results.snippet: San Jose leaders are delaying a vote on a plan to revamp a development program responsible for generating a significant portion of the city’s affordable housing. The change proposed by city staff would increase target income brackets for beneficiaries of San Jose’s inclusionary housing policy, which requires market-rate housing developers to direct a portion of their investments toward affordable housing. “We feel like any changes to a tool that is working well to produce affordability really needs to be thoroughly analyzed,” Alison Cingolani, a director for the San Jose-based affordable housing advocacy nonprofit SV@Home, told San José Spotlight. Typically, San Jose housing developers can fulfill the city’s inclusionary requirement by designating 15% of the homes in a residential project as below-market-rate affordable properties. #### San Jose apartment project to reduce affordable housing. #### San Jose seeks to squeeze more affordable housing out of developers. San Jose’s inclusionary housing requirement has helped produce about 288 affordable homes each year on average since 2019, according to city data.

top_results.snippet: In July 1979, the City Council adopted a rent stabilization ordinance for mobilehome parks and apartments, and created the City's Rental Dispute Program (now known as the Rent Stabilization Program) to administer the Ordinance. In 1985 the City Council voted to separate the Rent Stabilization Ordinance into two ordinances - one for mobilehome parks and another for apartments. Properties covered by San José's Apartment Rent Ordinance include apartments with three or more units that were built and occupied prior to September 7, 1979. Rental housing developments **exempted** from the ordinance include single-family homes, in-law units/granny flats/accessory dwelling units, duplexes, condominiums, townhomes, hotels, boarding houses rented to transient guests for periods of less than 30 days, nonprofit homes for the aged, school dormitories, rental units owned and operated by any government agency, any rental units first rented after September 7, 1979, and properties in unincorporated areas of San José. Under the Apartment Rent Ordinance, the maximum allowable rent increase is one 5% increase in a 12-month period.

top_results.snippet: Direct the City Manager to pursue the actions necessary to convert the 1 Branham Lane Emergency Interim Housing Community into permanent housing. I am grateful for the Mayor’s commitment to “continue to expand the supply of permanent housing options available for low-income residents,” and I support the direction in the March Budget Message affirming the Housing Department’s work to “evaluate the most cost- and time-effective pathway to convert select sites to permanent low-income housing, such as Single Room Occupancy units or other permanent low-income housing.” Over the past year, my office has worked with a dedicated Community Advisory Committee (CAC), including neighbors, City staff, and LifeMoves staff, to support this EIHC and address concerns. Although I am proud of the work performed by the CAC and grateful to the staff who participated for their time and diligence, I recommend that we begin the work to transition 1 Branham Lane from interim housing to permanent housing serving low-acuity low-income households.

top_results.snippet: The City of Santa Clara is committed to ensuring availability of affordable housing to moderate (Mod), low (LI), very-low (VLI) and extremely-low (ELI) income households through the adoption of the Affordable Housing Ordinance and Impact Fees. The City's Affordable Housing Ordinance includes a combination of inclusionary requirements for residential projects and impact fees for smaller projects and nonresidential projects to provide a steady stream of affordable housing units and income to fund the provision of affordable housing across the City. For nonresidential and residential developments (less than 10 units), the city charges an affordable housing impact fee as published in the Municipal Fee Schedule. 4. top_results.snippet: Our City » City Council » Boards, Commissions, and Committees. # Rental Housing Committee. The Rental Housing Committee (RHC) is established by the voter-approved Community Stabilization and Fair Rent Act (CSFRA), and consists of five members and one alternate member. It is tasked with implementing and administering the CSFRA and the Mobile Home Rent Stabilization Ordinance (MHRSO). Pursuant to the CSFRA and the MHRSO, the Committee’s powers and duties include:. * Intervening as an interested party in litigation with respect to covered rental units, subject to City Council approval. * Any other duties necessary to administer and enforce the CSFRA and MHRSO. As of January 2025, the Rental Housing Committee generally meets the fourth Thursday of each month at 6 p.m. at the following location:. The Current Agenda and supporting documents will be posted 72 hours prior to the hearing date at mountainview.legistar.com. The Housing Department supports the Rental Housing Committee.
